title: Pollo en Mole Chicken Mole Sauce

seo.title: Authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make the best authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) with our easy step-by-step recipe. Rich, flavorful Mexican chicken mole sauce from scratch. Perfect for dinner!
seo.h1: Authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) Recipe
h2Headings: What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?
Ingredients You Need for Chicken Mole
Step-by-Step Instructions for Pollo en Mole
Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole
Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole
seo.imageAlt: Authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) served with rice and tortillas, garnished with sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, complex flavors of this homemade authentic Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole), a classic Mexican dish perfect for any occasion.

description: Pollo en Mole is a quintessential Mexican dish featuring tender chicken bathed in a rich, complex, and deeply flavorful mole sauce. This traditional recipe balances sweet, savory, and spicy notes, creating an unforgettable culinary experience. It is a true celebration of Mexican heritage, perfect for special occasions or a hearty family meal.

instructions.title: Cook the Chicken
instructions.text: In a large pot, combine chicken pieces, 1/2 chopped onion, 2 smashed garlic cloves, bay leaf, and chicken broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 25-30 minutes until chicken is cooked through. Remove chicken, reserving the broth. Set chicken aside to cool slightly, then shred or keep whole.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dried Chiles
instructions.text: Stem and seed the ancho, mulato, and pasilla chiles. Heat a dry skillet over medium heat and lightly toast the chiles for 30 seconds per side until fragrant. Be careful not to burn them. Place toasted chiles in a bowl and cover with hot water or some of the reserved chicken broth. Let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes until soft.

instructions.title: Toast Spices and Aromatics
instructions.text: In the same dry skillet, toast cumin seeds, coriander seeds, anise seeds, whole cloves, and cinnamon stick until fragrant, about 1-2 minutes. Remove and set aside. Add 1 tablespoon of oil to the skillet. Fry the 1/2 roughly chopped onion, 3 peeled garlic cloves, almonds, pumpkin seeds, and 2 tablespoons of sesame seeds until lightly golden and fragrant. Add the bread or tortilla slices and fry until crisp. Remove all ingredients and set aside.

instructions.title: Blend the Mole Paste
instructions.text: Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving the soaking liquid. In a blender, combine the rehydrated chiles, toasted spices, fried aromatics, nuts, seeds, bread, roasted tomato, and Mexican chocolate. Add about 1 cup of the reserved chicken broth or chile soaking liquid. Blend until a very smooth paste forms, adding more liquid as needed to achieve a thick, pourable consistency. Strain the mole paste through a fine-mesh sieve into a bowl, pressing with a spoon to extract all liquid, discarding any solids. This step is crucial for a smooth mole.

instructions.title: Simmer the Mole Sauce
instructions.text: Heat the remaining 1 tablespoon of vegetable oil or lard in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Carefully pour the strained mole paste into the hot oil. It will sizzle vigorously. Stir constantly for 5-7 minutes, allowing the mole to fry and deepen in color. This step is essential for developing the flavor. Gradually stir in 3-4 cups of the reserved chicken broth, a little at a time, until the sauce reaches your desired consistency. Bring to a gentle simmer, reduce heat to low, and cook for at least 20-30 minutes, stirring occasionally, allowing the flavors to meld. Season with salt to taste.

instructions.title: Combine Chicken and Mole
instructions.text: Add the cooked chicken pieces to the simmering mole sauce. Ensure the chicken is fully coated and heated through. Continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes to allow the chicken to absorb the rich flavors of the mole. Adjust seasoning if necessary.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Serve the Pollo en Mole hot, garnished with a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds. It pairs wonderfully with Mexican red rice and warm corn tortillas for scooping up the delicious sauce. Enjoy this authentic Mexican culinary masterpiece!

tips: For a deeper flavor, roast your chiles and other ingredients before rehydrating and blending. This adds a smoky complexity.
Mexican chocolate is key to authentic mole; it often contains cinnamon and other spices. If unavailable, use unsweetened chocolate and add a pinch of cinnamon.
Mole sauce improves with time. Make it a day ahead and gently reheat it before serving to allow the flavors to fully meld.
article:
Unveiling the heart of Mexican cuisine, Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole) stands as a culinary masterpiece, a dish that tantalizes the senses with its unparalleled complexity and depth of flavor. Far from a simple sauce, mole is a symphony of ingredients – chiles, spices, nuts, seeds, and even a hint of chocolate – meticulously blended to create a rich, velvety elixir that coats tender chicken to perfection. If you've ever dreamt of recreating the authentic taste of Mexico in your own kitchen, embarking on the journey of making traditional Chicken Mole is an incredibly rewarding experience. Prepare to immerse yourself in a world where sweet, smoky, earthy, and subtly spicy notes dance together in a harmonious celebration of flavor.

## What is Chicken Mole (Pollo en Mole)?

Chicken Mole, or Pollo en Mole, is more than just a dish; it's a cultural icon in Mexico, particularly the renowned Mole Poblano from Puebla. At its core, mole refers to a family of sauces, each distinct in its ingredients and regional origin, but all characterized by their incredible richness and a long list of components. The most famous, and often what people refer to when they say "mole," is Mole Poblano. This particular mole is celebrated for its deep reddish-brown hue and a flavor profile that balances the heat of various dried chiles with the sweetness of fruits, the nuttiness of seeds, the earthiness of spices, and a touch of dark chocolate.

The preparation of a classic Chicken Mole is a labor of love, a testament to the slow food movement, involving toasting, rehydrating, grinding, and simmering numerous ingredients. It's a dish steeped in history, often served during special occasions, celebrations, and holidays, symbolizing tradition and community. While the ingredient list might seem daunting, the process is incredibly satisfying, yielding a sauce that is thick, glossy, and profoundly flavorful, transforming simple chicken into an extraordinary meal.

## Ingredients You Need for Chicken Mole

Crafting an authentic Chicken Mole requires a specific array of ingredients, each playing a crucial role in building its complex flavor profile. Don't be intimidated by the length; sourcing these items is often easier than you think, especially in Mexican markets or the international aisle of larger supermarkets.

For the Chicken:
*   2-3 lbs bone-in, skin-on chicken pieces (thighs, drumsticks, or a mix)
*   1/2 onion, quartered
*   2 cloves garlic, smashed
*   Salt to taste
*   Water to cover

For the Mole Sauce:
*   **Dried Chiles:**
    *   4 Ancho chiles (for sweetness, fruitiness, and dark color)
    *   3 Mulato chiles (for smokiness, earthiness, and dark color)
    *   3 Pasilla chiles (for mild heat, fruitiness, and dark color)
    *   1-2 Chipotle chiles (optional, for extra smokiness and heat)
*   **Aromatics & Spices:**
    *   1/2 white onion, roughly chopped
    *   3 cloves garlic, peeled
    *   1/4 cup sesame seeds, plus extra for garnish
    *   1/4 cup slivered almonds
    *   1/4 cup pumpkin seeds (pepitas)
    *   1/4 cup raisins
    *   2 tablespoons lard or vegetable oil
    *   1/2 teaspoon whole cumin seeds
    *   1/2 teaspoon whole black peppercorns
    *   3 whole cloves
    *   1 small stick Mexican cinnamon (or 1/2 tsp ground cinnamon)
    *   1/4 teaspoon dried oregano
    *   1/4 teaspoon dried thyme
*   **Thickeners & Sweeteners:**
    *   2 corn tortillas, torn into pieces
    *   1 slice stale bread (bolillo or baguette), torn
    *   1-2 ounces Mexican chocolate (tablets, typically Ibarra or Abuelita brand)
    *   1 tablespoon piloncillo (Mexican brown sugar cone) or dark brown sugar
*   **Liquids:**
    *   4-6 cups reserved chicken broth (from cooking the chicken)
    *   Salt to taste

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Pollo en Mole

Making Chicken Mole is a journey of patience and careful execution. Follow these steps to create a truly authentic and delicious dish.

1.  **Cook the Chicken:** Place the chicken pieces in a large pot with the quartered onion, smashed garlic, and a generous pinch of salt. Cover with water and bring to a boil. Reduce heat, cover, and simmer for 30-40 minutes, or until the chicken is tender and cooked through. Remove the chicken, reserving the cooking liquid (broth). Strain the broth and set aside. Once cool enough to handle, remove the skin and bones from the chicken, shredding or chopping the meat into desired pieces.

2.  **Prepare the Chiles:** Carefully wipe the dried chiles clean. Remove the stems, seeds, and veins (wear gloves if you have sensitive skin, as some chiles can be spicy). Heat a dry comal or skillet over medium heat and toast the chiles for 30 seconds to 1 minute per side, just until fragrant and slightly pliable. Be careful not to burn them, as this will make the mole bitter. Immediately transfer the toasted chiles to a bowl, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for 20-30 minutes, until soft.

3.  **Toast Nuts, Seeds & Spices:** In the same dry skillet, toast the sesame seeds, almonds, and pumpkin seeds separately until lightly golden and fragrant. Set aside. Next, toast the cumin seeds, peppercorns, cloves, and cinnamon stick until aromatic, about 1 minute. Add these to the bowl with the toasted nuts and seeds.

4.  **Sauté Aromatics & Thickeners:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot or Dutch oven, heat the lard or oil over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and garlic and sauté until softened and lightly golden, about 5-7 minutes. Add the torn tortillas and stale bread, frying until crisp and golden. Remove everything from the pot and add to the bowl with the toasted nuts, seeds, and spices.

5.  **Blend the Mole Paste:** Drain the rehydrated chiles, reserving a little of the soaking liquid. In a blender, combine the softened chiles, the sautéed aromatics, nuts, seeds, spices, raisins, Mexican chocolate, and piloncillo. Add 2 cups of the reserved chicken broth. Blend until incredibly smooth. This may take several minutes, and you might need to add more broth or a little chile soaking liquid to achieve a fine, velvety consistency. If your blender isn't powerful enough, you might want to pass the blended mixture through a fine-mesh sieve.

6.  **Simmer the Mole Sauce:** In the same large pot used earlier, heat another tablespoon of lard or oil over medium heat. Carefully pour the blended mole paste into the hot fat. This step is crucial for developing the mole's deep flavor and color. Bring it to a simmer, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon to prevent sticking, for at least 15-20 minutes. The sauce will darken and thicken, and the flavors will meld beautifully.

7.  **Combine and Serve:** Gradually add the remaining reserved chicken broth to the simmering mole, stirring until you reach your desired consistency. It should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon. Season with salt to taste. Add the cooked chicken pieces to the mole sauce and simmer for another 10-15 minutes, allowing the chicken to absorb the rich flavors. Your homemade Chicken Mole is now ready to be enjoyed!

## Pro Tips for the Best Chicken Mole

Making a truly exceptional Chicken Mole involves attention to detail and a willingness to embrace the process. Here are some tips to elevate your dish:

*   **Don't Burn the Chiles:** This is perhaps the most critical rule. Toasting chiles brings out their flavor, but burning them will introduce an unpleasant bitterness that can ruin your mole. Toast them quickly until just fragrant and slightly pliable.
*   **Blend Until Silky Smooth:** The texture of your mole is key. A truly authentic mole is velvety and smooth. Blend for a long time, and if necessary, pass the finished sauce through a fine-mesh sieve to remove any stubborn bits of chile skin or seeds.
*   **Fry the Mole Paste:** Don't skip the step of frying the mole paste in hot fat. This process, known as "sazonar" or "freír," deepens the flavor, darkens the color, and helps the sauce thicken and develop its characteristic richness. Stir constantly to prevent sticking.
*   **Balance the Flavors:** Taste your mole as it simmers. Mole is all about balance. If it's too bitter, a little more chocolate or piloncillo can help. If it lacks depth, a pinch more salt or a prolonged simmer can work wonders.
*   **Make Ahead:** Mole sauce is one of those dishes that tastes even better the next day. The flavors continue to meld and deepen overnight. Prepare the sauce in advance and gently reheat it with the chicken just before serving.
*   **Quality Ingredients:** Use good quality dried chiles and authentic Mexican chocolate for the best results. These foundational ingredients dictate much of the final flavor.

## Serving Suggestions for Pollo en Mole

Chicken Mole is a hearty and satisfying dish on its own, but it truly shines when served with traditional accompaniments.

*   **Rice:** The most common pairing is simple white rice, which helps to absorb the rich sauce and provides a neutral canvas for the complex flavors of the mole. Mexican red rice is another excellent choice.
*   **Tortillas:** Warm corn tortillas are essential for scooping up every last drop of the luscious sauce. Flour tortillas can also be used.
*   **Garnish:** A sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds is a classic garnish, adding a nutty crunch. Chopped fresh cilantro or finely diced white onion can also be added for freshness.
*   **Side Dishes:** A simple side salad with a light vinaigrette can cut through the richness, or serve with a side of refried beans.

## Variations and Substitutions

While this recipe focuses on a classic Mole Poblano-style Chicken Mole, the world of mole is vast, offering many exciting variations.

*   **Other Proteins:** Mole pairs beautifully with other proteins. Try it with turkey (especially popular after Thanksgiving), pork shoulder, beef short ribs, or even plant-based options like roasted vegetables, firm tofu, or mushrooms for a vegetarian mole.
*   **Different Mole Types:** Explore other moles like Mole Negro (often from Oaxaca, even darker and richer), Mole Verde (a fresh, herb-based green mole), Mole Coloradito (a reddish mole with a slightly sweeter profile), or Mole Almendrado (almond mole). Each uses a different combination of chiles, herbs, and spices.
*   **Nut/Seed Substitutions:** If you have allergies or can't find specific nuts, you can experiment. Cashews or walnuts can sometimes stand in for almonds or peanuts, though the flavor profile will subtly change.
*   **Chocolate Adjustments:** If Mexican chocolate is unavailable, you can use unsweetened dark chocolate (70-80% cacao) and adjust the amount of piloncillo or brown sugar to achieve the desired sweetness.
*   **Heat Level:** For more heat, add a few chiles de árbol to your blend. For less, reduce the number of chipotle chiles or omit them entirely.

Crafting Chicken Mole is an culinary adventure that connects you to the rich heritage of Mexican cuisine. The effort is undoubtedly worth it, as the reward is a dish of incredible depth, warmth, and unforgettable flavor that will impress anyone lucky enough to try it. Enjoy the process, savor the aromas, and delight in the complex magic of Pollo en Mole.
